title: The connection between hormonal disruption and childhood trauma
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
Your body keeps score. But what if it also keeps your hormones hostage?

htmlContent:
Your body keeps score. But what if it also keeps your hormones hostage?

I remember sitting in a doctor's office at twenty-nine, exhausted in a way that sleep couldn't touch. My periods had gone haywire. My thyroid labs looked like a roller coaster. The doctor ran more tests, frowned at the results, and said something I've never forgotten. "Your cortisol is through the roof. Your estrogen is a mess. Have you ever been through something traumatic?"

I laughed. Not because it was funny. Because I had spent years convincing myself my childhood "wasn't that bad."

Here's the thing. Your nervous system and your endocrine system are not separate. They are not even two systems that simply "talk to each other." They are woven together, cell by cell, hormone by hormone. When trauma happens early, especially in childhood, it doesn't just leave emotional scars. It rewires the entire hormonal architecture of your body. And that rewiring shows up in ways you might never connect back to your past.

Think about it. Chronic fatigue. Unexplained weight gain. Thyroid disorders. PCOS. Early menopause. Adrenal burnout. These are not random. They are often the body's hormonal cry for help, rooted in a nervous system that learned, years ago, that the world was not safe.

Stay with me here. This is not about blaming yourself. It's about understanding the connection so you can actually do something about it.

The stress response that never turned off

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how your nervous system responds to threat. His work shows that when you grow up in an unpredictable or dangerous environment, your body stays in a state of high alert. Not because you choose to. Because your survival depends on it.

Here's what happens biologically. Your hypothalamus, the tiny control center in your brain, detects threat and signals your pituitary gland. Your pituitary tells your adrenal glands to release cortisol and adrenaline. This is the stress response, and it's designed to be temporary. Run from the tiger, survive, then rest.

But childhood trauma doesn't work like a single tiger encounter. It's chronic. It's unpredictable. It's the parent who rages one day and ignores you the next. It's the absence of safety, the feeling that you are alone, the terror that never fully leaves. And your body, brilliant and adaptive, does the only thing it can. It keeps the stress response running. Permanently.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown through decades of research that this chronic stress activation literally changes your brain and body. Your HPA axis, the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal pathway, becomes dysregulated. Cortisol either stays too high, flooding your system, or it crashes, leaving you depleted. Your sex hormones, estrogen, progesterone, testosterone, get pushed aside. The body prioritizes survival over reproduction. That makes perfect sense from an evolutionary standpoint. But it wreaks havoc on your hormonal health.

I see this in women who have PCOS and a history of abuse. I see it in men with low testosterone who grew up in chaotic homes. I see it in people whose thyroid stopped working properly after years of unprocessed stress. The pattern is unmistakable. Trauma disrupts hormones. And hormones disrupt everything.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is "In an Unspoken Voice" (paid link). Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, walks through how the body holds trauma and how gentle, titrated movement can release it. It's not about reliving the worst moments. It's about letting your nervous system know, slowly, that the danger is over.

The estrogen connection you didn't see coming

Let's talk about estrogen for a minute. Not as a standalone hormone, but as a player in a much larger story.

Estrogen is not just about reproduction. It affects your brain, your mood, your bone density, your immune system, your cardiovascular health. And it is deeply sensitive to stress. When cortisol stays high, your body converts more of your hormones into cortisol precursors. That means less estrogen available for its normal functions. You end up with what some researchers call "pseudo-estrogen deficiency." Your labs might look normal, but your cells aren't getting what they need.

This can show up as heavy periods, skipped periods, PMS that feels unbearable, or early perimenopause. I have talked to women in their early thirties who were told they were "prematurely menopausal" and given hormone replacement without anyone asking about their trauma history. Not that HRT is wrong. But it addresses the symptom, not the root.

Gabor Mate, a physician who has written extensively about trauma and illness, says something that sticks with me. "The question is not why the disease, but why this person, at this time, in these circumstances." He connects childhood adversity directly to autoimmune conditions, chronic fatigue, and hormonal disorders. His book "When the Body Says No" (paid link) explores how suppressed stress and unexpressed emotion accumulate in the body, often leading to illness years later. It's a hard read because it asks you to look at your own patterns. But it's worth it.

I want to be clear about something. This is not about saying your hormonal problems are "all in your head." They are in your body. They are real. They are measurable. But the cause might not be where you think it is. And that's actually good news, because it means there is a path forward that doesn't require you to medicate yourself into numbness or chase symptoms forever.

What about men? The hidden story of testosterone and trauma

We don't talk enough about how childhood trauma affects male hormones. But it does. Deeply.

Testosterone is often framed as the "male hormone," but it's present in all bodies and affects energy, libido, muscle mass, mood, and cognitive function. Chronic stress suppresses testosterone. Cortisol and testosterone are antagonists. When one goes up, the other goes down. And in men who experienced childhood trauma, the pattern is clear. Lower testosterone, higher cortisol, and a higher risk of depression, anxiety, and metabolic issues.

I have worked with men who spent years in the gym, lifting heavy, eating clean, doing everything "right," and still felt flat. No drive. No spark. No sense of vitality. They assumed something was wrong with their genetics or their discipline. But when we looked at their history, the picture emerged. Chaotic homes. Emotional neglect. Physical punishment. The body had learned that safety meant being small, being invisible, being compliant. And testosterone, the hormone of assertion and vitality, got turned down as a survival strategy.

This is not about blaming parents or wallowing in victimhood. It's about seeing the pattern so you can interrupt it. Your body adapted to keep you alive. That adaptation was brilliant. But it might not be serving you anymore.

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is the TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link). I know it sounds simple, but working with the fascia and muscles where tension accumulates can signal the nervous system that it's safe to let go. It's not a cure. But it's a piece of the puzzle.

Regulation as the foundation for hormonal healing

So what do you actually do about this? You cannot think your way out of a dysregulated nervous system. You cannot positive-affirm your way out of high cortisol. You cannot supplement your way out of a body that still believes it is in danger. The work has to start at the level of the nervous system.

Regulation means teaching your body, over and over, that it is safe now. Not that the past didn't happen. Not that you are "over it." But that in this moment, your nervous system can downshift from survival mode into connection and repair.

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in Polyvagal Theory, talks about "glimmers." Small moments of safety that you can train yourself to notice. The warmth of sunlight on your skin. The sound of a friend's laugh. The feeling of your feet on the ground. These are not trivial. They are the building blocks of a regulated nervous system. And a regulated nervous system supports healthy hormone production.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). Extended exhales activate the vagus nerve, which is the main highway of the parasympathetic nervous system. Even a few minutes a day can shift your baseline.

I'm not going to tell you this is easy. It's not. Rebuilding hormonal health after childhood trauma takes time. It takes patience. It takes a willingness to feel things you have been avoiding for years. But it is possible. I have seen it happen. I have lived it myself.

The connection between hormonal disruption and childhood trauma is not a theory. It is a biological reality that thousands of researchers have documented. And once you see it, you cannot unsee it. You start to understand why your body has been struggling. And you start to understand that it was never your fault.

Sit with that.
